This has information which is a starting point for each guild

Teacher's guild

**

Hello, pedagogues (apparently pedagogues has a negative connotation - is that true?). While this group will have many teachers in it, there are many with skills focused on the design of educational resources who are not teachers. That is why this group is called pedagogy, the skills of writing and designing good educational materials. 

Our primary focus is the design of the Narrow Way app. There are two sides to this. How to write and sequence the resources well and what is the best user interface (UI) for the app. 

The main question is, "What is best practice in educational UI"? Particularly for spiritual formation. What is best practice in spiritual formation resources?

VIDEO RESEARCH

There is some excellent research into video education (by my colleague [Dr Matt Fyfield](https://www.youtube.com/@mattfyfield))

Fyfield, Matt & Henderson, Michael & Phillips, Michael. (2022). [25 principles for effective instructional video design.](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/364625539_25_principles_for_effective_instructional_video_design) ASCILITE Publications. 418-423. 10.14742/apubs.2019.299. 

Fyfield, M., Henderson, M., & Phillips, M. (2022). [Improving instructional video design: A systematic review.](https://ajet.org.au/index.php/AJET/article/view/7296) Australasian Journal of Educational Technology, 38(3), 155--183. <https://doi.org/10.14742/ajet.7296>

Fyfield, Matt & Henderson, Michael & Heinrich, Eva & Redmond, Petrea. (2019). [Videos in higher education: Making the most of a good thing.](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/337447143_Videos_in_higher_education_Making_the_most_of_a_good_thing) Australasian Journal of Educational Technology. 35. 1-7. 10.14742/ajet.5930. 

Who has the goods on educational UI? 

GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EDUCATION

This article apparently  is 'as best as it gets': [Principles of Instruction: Research-Based Strategies That All Teachers Should Know, by Barak Rosenshine; American Educator Vol. 36, No. 1, Spring 2012, AFT (teachertoolkit.co.uk)](https://www.teachertoolkit.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/Principles-of-Insruction-Rosenshine.pdf) Is there anything better?

Any other resources?

The most effective teachers ensured that their students efficiently acquired, rehearsed, and connected background knowledge by providing a good deal of instructional support. They provided this support by teaching new material in manageable amounts, modeling, guiding student practice, helping students when they made errors, and providing for sufficient practice and review.

1.  Begin a lesson with a short review of previous learning.

2.  Present new material in small steps with student practice after each step.

3.  Ask a large number of questions and check the responses of all students.

4.  Provide models.

5.  Guide student practice.

6.  Check for student understanding.

7.  Obtain a high success rate.

8.  Provide sca-olds for di/cult tasks.

9.  Require and monitor independent practice.

10.  Engage students in weekly and monthly review.

**


Coders Guild

**Welcome to the coders group. This group contains people selecting the following from the original form: 

Technical skills such as programming, coding, web/app design.

Computer skills. such as navigating apps, good with software.

Workflows/Workflow testing.**


**NWA TRIALING GUILD**

**

Welcome to the Narrow Way app trialing group. This is where the rubber hits the road. Your contribution is invaluable for the success of this project. Please God, you will see growth in yourself and those you invite into trialing these resources.

We now have an automated way to join the trialling system. Just complete this [form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeqbo_ijo9XNRTS3daqTUznmmZtpE-8tfGup8oEFqtQSW25Sg/viewform?usp=sf_link) and you will be added to the system. You have many choices. How to use the system is explained here.

Please read this guide first (But don't complete it): [INTRODUCTION GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RzRhB_VZ6BLm5d7n66q3QsfU6asa3Md1rQuHvyPcF5k/edit)

Please complete this application [The Narrow Way testing application](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EC1sbD92egvheNmEkl3YTjGgtDni6Cxi7xVq39Tzrps/edit) 

To mentor others, please use this application (If you think it can be improved, then please comment in the document) [Narrow Way app mentor application](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1JbBaby2AnTFf7caGiQ4eJ6BKiY2c4mkXK73QAt11twY/edit)

As you use the system, please provide feedback (comments, suggestions) so it can be improved. 

You can also log any errors in the system here: [AVC Error report form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSd_j0VMpDrlGH9-MHOo43QWpRrxaKtKMagY7nrsE80hxzoapA/viewform?usp=sf_link)

You can also make suggestions for:

[NWA Functionality](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1yUHdtGOIeFr5pEEgDJ3OIMSefPft6uqZ5TJI1EejAUQ/edit)

[NWA Design (UI)](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11UEJLm0c3JUpxKR1xtW-iICx5JajausM5GHdcrjFJbY/edit)

Thanks for helping out!

These pages are the content for the above links.

INTRODUCTION GUIDE v3

{{Group Progress}} | [Master Document](https://docs.google.com/document/d/18SX5rxLRhjRl5NAk7rrRn_pJOn1cbMsHlxe1yOa0PW0)

Welcome! You are invited to grow in holiness and make the process to grow in holiness better for others.

All things for God, begin before God on our knees. Please say a prayer for this project.

This particular infrastructure is called The Narrow Way App (until we get a better name). It is part of a process to take the wisdom of the saints and share it with the world. It is a way to harness the best formation from the Apostoli Viae community and the Avila Institute and package it into a set of interactive resources for formation in a growing community of accountability. This collaborative resource development will be integrated into a website called AV Commons. One tool for accessing the resources will be the Holy Habits app. The Narrow Way App is a set of Google docs and sheets which have Google code embedded in them to provide the group leader with functionality to facilitate a group to grow in holiness and give feedback and suggestions to improve the resources.

Your feedback and suggestions are shared as public domain, which means you license all your feedback and suggestions in a way that allows it to be shared with the world and even sold. There will be no acknowledgement of your contributions. As contributions are integrated into the resources all user data is scrubbed. That also means you can share all the resources created for free and do with them what you like. You are free to use them for evangelisation any way you are so inspired. [This video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q0VzUigrb_g) explains it well. Books and ebooks integrated into The Narrow Way App such as Into the Deep by Dan Burke are not free. We respect copyright.

The way each guide works is that it is a google doc which has been set up so anyone can make comments. That is the primary way you contribute. Think something can be written better? Go ahead and make a comment. Or copy the whole document, turn on suggestions and go for your life. Then you can send your copy in. At times there will be alternatives given - a choice between two versions of the same document. You get to vote and provide feedback on which one you think is better and why. All of this feedback is invaluable to improve it. All for one and one for all.

Often we don't know what is the right book for us to read that will help us to grow in holiness. Then we might read it and not really put it into practice. Often we are just trying to grow in our own efforts without anyone to support us. The Narrow Way App fixes all three through resources, community and functionality.

The Conditions Necessary for Spiritual Growth

Clarity, community and accountability are necessary for spiritual growth. The Narrow Way provides all three. You are part of a small group which is your community. The Narrow Way is a set of resources that will establish the key disciplines and formation for holiness, starting with the first resource Into the Deep by Dan Burke on mental prayer. The key functionality of the Narrow Way is accountability. You will be contacted daily through an automated messaging system (SMS or email) to encourage you to keep making daily progress. This will allow you to respond with the progress you have made which is recorded and visible to your group.

You will have your own copy of the resource, eg [Into the Deep Guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Dpx_z60k3oYhnzhBuhBLWZDh_iyMVgaj88VgKNwJck4/edit).

There is a group progress document you can access eg Group Progress 123456.

You can make comments on the current Master Document, eg [Into the Deep Guidebook](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PSh3pX_qw73xODCJU9RtlBuD6fXHmVQdtMoQFfywu8U/edit), which is the primary way you provide feedback.

There are also surveys you can complete. These comments can be anonymous.

Most importantly, this is a chance to grow in holiness and just learn and put into practice what you are learning and to enjoy the journey with your friends. 

Each guide will have some tasks to complete. The idea is to make each task simple enough and take about 8 minutes to complete. You can go through a document as fast or as slow as you like. Aim is to complete at least one task a day.

We now have a set of simple tasks for you to practice all the skills for you to make the most of this process. 

Each guidebook has particular steps that you can tick off. Like this one.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Practicing ticking off a task.

Go ahead and tick it (If you are on a phone you will need to turn edit mode on by clicking on the pencil in the bottom right. When finished click the tick in the top left). Well done! One small tick in this Google doc, one giant leap for your spiritual progress. Next task is to make a comment.

You should now make a comment for my lame reference to the moon landing. 

For those on a laptop: Highlight that sentence and you will see three icons appear on the right. The top one is for comments. Click on it and leave a comment. Then tick the box below since you have now made a comment.

For those on a phone: Turn on edit mode by clicking on the pencil bottom right. Highlight the sentence and you will see a little menu appear with three dots on the right. Click on the three dots and choose add comment. Click on it and leave a comment. Then tick the box below since you have now made a comment.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Make a comment.

Time to make a really cool, hip .... suggestion. 

For those on a laptop: Highlight this sentence, and click on the bottom icon which will turn on suggestion mode. Then edit the '....' in the first sentence of the paragraph and add you own 'cool' type of word, eg 'extraordinary'. Then turn off suggestion mode by changing the icon towards the top right to 'editing' mode. Then tick the 'Make a suggestion' box since you have completed that task too.

For those on a phone: Click on the three dots on the top right of the screen. Change "Suggest changes" to on. This will turn on suggestion mode which will track all your changes. Then edit the '....' in the first sentence of the paragraph and add you own 'cool' type of word, eg 'extraordinary'. Then turn off suggestion mode the same way by clicking on those three dots in the top right and changing "Suggest changes" to off. Then tick the 'Make a suggestion' box since you have completed that task too.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Make a suggestion.

Complete [this survey](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScY1J26jj7kYiT3cua518yBzoBYYxm2OcBhesHqv-TE_pS3hg/viewform?usp=sf_link) on this guide and then tick the box below.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Complete a survey

Well done! You've completed your first guide and learnt how to contribute your ideas to help lead others in holiness.

NWA Functionality

This document describes the functionality for the Narrow Way App. Please knock yourself out contributing to this document.

My current thinking is this (Please add your own thoughts/ideas using the 'Suggesting' option top right, or just comment (highlight some text and choose the comment icon on the right):

1.  The aim is to grow in holiness.

2.  The spiritual life is broken down into particular disciplines based on the best resources available. Here is the initial map of those resources: [Spiritual Disciplines Map](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lTNeSvLPK9zM5XqMuCnx1_JeaSm596FdVkDy_mqXJpw/edit). 

3.  Here is the first resource as a starting point: [INTO THE DEEP GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qbNad_Lo6_lrlGbkVnRmZ63bC0ilRFAIFPj0sdh3PvU/edit). But there could be other alternatives, eg divine mercy chaplet, Rosary, some other prayer?

4.  The core 

5.  The way I am imaging it working is:

1.  You make your way through one guide at a time.

2.  The guide has checkboxes to make the person accountable to completing the various resolutions based on the wisdom of the book. For example, the simplest is to just read a number of pages. For into the deep it could be to create a prayer corner. Each guide would have its own set of tasks.

3.  These checkboxes allow the person to monitor their own progress, but also to share it with others to be accountable/mentored. 

4.  You can go as fast or as slow as you like, generally, one checkbox per day is good and should be how it is designed/written.

5.  The key is simplicity. The content that goes with each checkbox is about 8 minutes of reading. That way it is manageable for everyone. As long as the person keeps reading that 8 minutes per day and putting it into practice, they will continue to grow.

1.  This start with just reading through Into the Deep, but then gradually start actually praying with 5 minutes per day. Over time this would build up to establish a regular time of mental prayer. Once that is consolidated, then the next disciple can be actioned.

2.  This is probably the most crucial aspect of the whole design. How to keep the interest going and keep building. This is going to take a lot of thought, research, trialing etc. It could be that the app works differently for different people. There is some kind of way the app is able to work out what works best for the person and tailors itself to that methodology.

3.  Accountability is usually the key way that interest is kept on track. Having multiple ways for accountability to work so the app can be useful in multiple contexts. Eg weekly report to spiritual director/mentor. Reminders via txt/email other means? Printout reminder to be posted next to mirror/ put on the toilet wall. An image that could be printed onto a mug so the person's cup is a reminder... Any other ideas... The system has a way to capture and share all these ideas that could be added by anyone. Everytime they miss a day, a dollar is donated to the Avila foundation. (please note: I at times add ideas for comic relief).

7.  Once a guide has been completed, options are given for the next guide to be chosen. 

8.  There is a process for selecting the next guide. This would involve reviewing previous accomplishments and thanking the Lord for them. Being introduced to each possible new guide. A short catechesis on spiritual growth. A time of prayer and reflection to decide. The decision. A prayer asking for strength to complete the next guide.

9.  The short catechesis on spiritual growth is a crucial element. It is where a pep talk is given orienting us to being docile to the Holy Spirit. Key points such as

1.  Inch by inch is a cinch, yard by yard is too hard.

2.  It is not about any particular discipline, but about being docile to the Holy Spirit

3.  Our spiritual life must help us live our state in life better if it is to be authentic.

4.  Feel free to add more points here.

11.  There can be other features added.

1.  There could be a quiz using [h5p tools](https://h5p.org/content-types-and-applications) integrated into the app.

2.  There can be a summary of all the content.

3.  There can be alternate versions for different audiences, eg young people, mothers, professionals, etc.

4.  There can be a review version for those who have already have the discipline but need to check if they have mastery or not.

5.  There can be a 'pro tips' version that come from approved suggestions made by users. Eg if starting a new habit, try to connect it with a key event in the day, eg when I get first home from work, or when I first go to my room after dinner.

13.  To be inclusive, all the resources are available to be printed or used in the app. So people can make use of the formation without ever using their phone. Possibly these guides can be small booklets which could be available in Church foyers.

**


**

NWA Design (UI)

This document follows on from the functional requirements: [NWA Functionality](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1yUHdtGOIeFr5pEEgDJ3OIMSefPft6uqZ5TJI1EejAUQ/edit)

Once we have some clarity around the functional requirements, we will start on the NWA UI. For now please check out Holy Habits ([android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.holyhabits&hl=en_US), [iphone](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/holy-habits-everyday-sanctity/id6448125135)) as a starting point. If you want to have a go at working on it I suggest using figma.

Want to learn/sharpen your design skills: <https://m3.material.io/> 

People could use [figma](https://www.figma.com/) or [wireframepro](https://workspace.google.com/marketplace/app/wireframepro/413107853716).

A QUICK WAY

A quick way to do design today is using chatgpt 4+ . Put in a screen shot of the UI and chatgpt will give you the code, eg "Please give me react code and css to replicate what you see here in this design". Drago of Holy Habits is happy to run a session about all this with us. Please pray for him (ask Rob Zaar to make this happen <rjzaar@gmail.com>).

The process, I suggest is to start with a blank sheet of actual paper and design what you think would be the ideal UI design for the NWA. You might like to look at various similar apps for ideas. Then share it with us. (Put a link below)

LINKS TO POSSIBLE DESIGNS:

{put links here}

Then look at other people's designs. We will work together to put together a final specification to be coded.

One of the keys is to make the app integrated into the way someone does things and track the spiritual disciplines.

Initially vocal prayers are important in the spiritual life since they provide an easily sustainable backbone for prayer consistency. It is easy to start and finish a vocal prayer. If the vocal prayers are in the app then the app can track when they are prayed. Alternatively, the app could provide a digital candle to accompany the prayer. Different candles for different prayers? Or a focal image (imago divina) that is clicked to be selected and indicates that that prayer (or set of prayers) is prayed. Prayer alarms is another possibility. Or launching a different prayer app (is that possible)?

**

WRITERS GUILD

I am sure you writers could improve my prose in this explanation. The writers group covers a range of skills, which could possibly be split into different groups. The application form has three relevant options:

-   Technical writing and/or editing experience.

-   Spiritual writing; i.e., writing guides for books such as Spiritual Warfare and the Discernment of Spirits

-   Pedagogy. (Improving how something is explained and taught). Teachers are suited to this group.

The first two flow into this group.

The last one flows into its own separate group since it has particular skills in terms of design.

Currently I perceive three skill sets

1.  Proofreading any document that is written for spelling, grammar, style.

2.  Writing new spiritual guides from scratch.

3.  Creating writing guidelines.

CREATING WRITING GUIDELINES

Writing guidelines are now in this doc: [TNW Guide Writing guidelines](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1d-gqF59R5J-Hqc1kOSOOlXvM25k3UvQkpSU1IoKiLIo/edit) which still needs work.

PROOFREADING

So writers, please have a go at all the documents in the system and suggesting improvements. Only use comments and suggested edits. This [introductory guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RzRhB_VZ6BLm5d7n66q3QsfU6asa3Md1rQuHvyPcF5k/edit) from the Narrow Way app explains it.

Please proofread what has already been written:

[INTRODUCTION GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RzRhB_VZ6BLm5d7n66q3QsfU6asa3Md1rQuHvyPcF5k/edit)

[INTO THE DEEP GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qbNad_Lo6_lrlGbkVnRmZ63bC0ilRFAIFPj0sdh3PvU/edit)

[Spiritual Disciplines Map](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lTNeSvLPK9zM5XqMuCnx1_JeaSm596FdVkDy_mqXJpw/edit)

NEW SPIRITUAL GUIDES

Please review/write the following guide

[Spiritual Warfare and the Discernment of Spirits Guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/12HLMs-Z-RqmAo9n35jIzaMVsP_yrATlzdC1Ec56m2mM/edit)

**

**

TNW Guide Writing guidelines

BACKGROUND

Currently there are two guides written, though the first is introductory. 

[INTRODUCTION GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RzRhB_VZ6BLm5d7n66q3QsfU6asa3Md1rQuHvyPcF5k/edit)

[INTO THE DEEP GUIDE](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qbNad_Lo6_lrlGbkVnRmZ63bC0ilRFAIFPj0sdh3PvU/edit)

And a third that needs to be written. Of course, over time, there will be many more guides to be written over time once these have been identified as the [Spiritual Disciplines Map](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lTNeSvLPK9zM5XqMuCnx1_JeaSm596FdVkDy_mqXJpw/edit) is refined.  

[Spiritual Disciplines Map](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lTNeSvLPK9zM5XqMuCnx1_JeaSm596FdVkDy_mqXJpw/edit)

Reading and using them will help get the idea of what works and what could work better.

This writing guide will be a combined effort of pedagogues and writers since it incorporates skills of both.

WRITING GUIDE

These points are just to get the ball rolling.

1.  The general key principle is to extract from the text, eg Into the Deep practical steps that a reader should put into practice, eg create a prayer corner. These will be checkboxes.

2.  The guide should be broken down into parts that take about 7-8 minutes to read which itself can be a checkbox. This allows the guide to be gradually digested and is achievable (theoretically) by anyone on a day to day basis.

3.  There can be a variety of tasks

1.  Some are single tasks, eg read pages 20-24.

2.  Some tasks might take time, eg create a prayer corner.

3.  Some tasks might be daily tasks, eg pray 10 minutes a day.

4.  Some tasks might be part of a review, eg how is the resolution part of mental prayer going.

5.  Any other thoughts?

5.  There can be a variety of styles to the same resource, eg it would be written differently for young adults compared to single mothers.

6.  How should it be aimed? How persuasively? Should be hooks? Quotes? Encouragingly? 

7.  How much background should be included? How much technical language? A glossary?

8.  What level of background knowledge should be assumed?

9.  Any other questions points included?

**

**

INTO THE DEEP GUIDE v2

{{Group Progress}} | [Instructions](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qwnGcK5E1bp-nEVBfz1yfdnE62Xo8ajCNoWCP8UucJQ/edit?usp=sharing) | [Master document](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PSh3pX_qw73xODCJU9RtlBuD6fXHmVQdtMoQFfywu8U/edit?usp=sharing)

Into the Deep Guide and Checkpoints

This is a practice guide to help you appropriate the wisdom of the book Into the Deep by Dan Burke. Each checkpoint is explained. There is a single page checklist you can use to check off your journey through this adventure. There are three types of checkpoints which are colour coded for your convenience:

Tasks: Tasks are once off things you do. You can revisit them if need be.

Skills: Skills are things that can take time to work on to master. Each one of these need to be mastered before moving on.

Habits: Habits take longer to develop and should be reflected on regularly to stay on track.

You can make your progress as fast or as slow as you wish. There is single page at the end of this guide that you can use to check off your checkpoints. Tasks are just checked off once done. Skills and habits take longer and so are started, but not completed until consolidated. Once a skill or habit has been started, then continue to make progress through the rest of the checkpoints.

INTRODUCTION: WHY PRAY?

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read the Introduction p. 3-7.

ONE: YOUR DESIRE FOR GOD, GOD'S DESIRE FOR YOU. 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p.11-15

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read the prayer on page 12 slowly out loud.

TWO: DISCOVERY PRAYER

If you begin now, even if you struggle a bit, you will be better prepared for what is to come: 

Reading: Performing an attentive, slow, leisurely, and repetitious reading of a short passage of the Bible 

Reflecting: Making an effort to prayerfully engage with the meaning of the passage and to consider how it may apply to your life circumstances 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p. 19-24

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Simply set aside ten minutes a day and begin to practice the easiest of these steps --- reading and reflecting.

THREE: ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS FOR PROGRESS IN PRAYER

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p. 27-31

Sacred Time: Schedule your prayer time at the beginning of each day, before you schedule anything else. View your prayer time as sacred and nonnegotiable. Once you choose the time, promise God that you will protect it and treat it as a priority. 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Choose a prayer time and stick with it.

Sacred Space: It is ideal to create a space dedicated to prayer and nothing else. You can use any suitable surface for a favorite icon, candle, or holy image; for example, a quiet, unused corner space with a small shelf. My first prayer space was a very simple combination of a windowsill, an icon, a candle, and a small bench. Be sure that your prayer space is peaceful and devoid of anything that might draw your attention or distract you ( computers, phones, TV, iThings, etc. ). 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Set up a sacred space.

Sacred Attention: Once your environment is properly prepared, make sure your posture is conducive to peaceful prayer and reading, recognizing that you are entering into the presence of God. 

Now it is time to turn your heart to God. Begin to breathe slowly and deeply, focusing on simple attentiveness to Jesus as you seek to turn to him in Scripture. If your mind wanders --- and it will until the day you die --- don't allow for frustration or self - condemnation; simply and gently bring your attention back to God and the text. 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Use attentive posture, bring your attention back to God and the text.

FOUR: MAKING A SOLEMN COMMITMENT TO GOD

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p. 35-39

Now that you understand the basic steps and the essential elements of prayer, it is time to make an informed, purposeful, and meaningful commitment to God. Ensure that those goals are realistic, clear, concrete, specific, and time bound. 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Make a solemn commitment to God according to the suggestion in this chapter.

FIVE: STEP-BY-STEP INTO THE PRESENCE OF GOD

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p.43-57

Choose your sequence of Gospel passages for your prayer time.

1.  Daily Mass Gospel passage

2.  One of the four gospels sequentially.

STEP ONE: READING 

1.   Enter your sacred space. 

2.  Be sure you leave behind anything that might distract you during this time you have dedicated to God. Avoid engaging with technology right before you pray. 

3.  Greet the Lord. 

4.  Slowly and gently pray an opening prayer out loud. Consider the meaning of each word as you pray as you say it. Ensure you are directing your attention to God as you pray. 

5.  Begin with an attentive, slow reading of a short passage of Scripture in the Gospels. 

6.  The key is not to rush. The goal is not to finish a particular portion of Scripture --- or even to finish the steps laid out in Discovery Prayer --- but to purposefully delve into the depths of any passage that will draw your heart and mind to God. Said another way, this method is meant to orient your mind and heart to God, and you can set aside the method when this happens during any given time in prayer. 

Ways to deepen your prayer time

1.  As you begin, you might also follow a beautiful tradition and trace the sign of the cross on the Scriptures, kiss the cross you traced, and then begin to read very slowly and gently --- out loud --- seeking to absorb the words themselves along with any related ideas and images that surface from each word. 

2.  When a particular passage or word strikes you, pause to consider it more fully. As you pause, you will then naturally move into step two, reflecting. If you don't seem to progress in the way suggested by this method, simply continue reading slowly and even reread the passage. 

3.  As a general rule, each selected short (as short as possible ) portion of Scripture should be read aloud slowly three times before you move on to the next section. Each of us moves at our own pace, however. The goal is not to mechanically execute the method, but to honor and seek God. 

4.  When you read at your natural or normal pace, you are reading for information. But this prayerful reading is meant to help you encounter Jesus, listen to his voice, and follow his lead.

STEP TWO: REFLECTING 

In this second step, you engage with the details, words, places, visual images, attitudes, and insights of the passage, seeking to fully absorb and understand it and then apply it to your own life. 

Gently and peacefully ponder what you have read, visualizing it and listening carefully for the Holy Spirit's prompting or guidance. Look for the deeper spiritual meanings of the words as you place yourself in a Gospel scene as one of the participants, or simply hear God speaking directly to you as you read the words. 

To slow the process down and avoid slipping into the habit of reading at your normal pace, it can be helpful to briefly pause after each word before going on to the next one. As you do this, you will break out of your usual reading pattern, and you will make room for silence and careful listening. Your goal is to disrupt the typical frantic pace of life in order to be attentive to God, as you would with your most intimate companion. 

STEP THREE: RESPONDING 

As you are drawn into a particular passage, begin to converse with God about what you are reading, and seek to respond with your heart. You can ask for forgiveness, you can thank him and praise him, or you can ask him for the grace to be changed by what you have read. You can ask him to help you more fully realize what he wants you to be, and you can ask for his help in applying his moral, spiritual, or practical guidance to your life. 

STEP FOUR: RESTING 

Allow yourself to become absorbed in God's words as he invites you into a deeper kind of prayer, one that will bring you into his presence in ways that purely mental exercises could never achieve. You may not experience this kind of absorption as you initially explore this method of prayer. 

STEP FIVE: RESOLVING 

This final step involves making a clear resolution so you avoid a dangerous trap in the spiritual life: encountering God and doing nothing in response. One of the great spiritual masters of the Church said this about how we should respond when God reveals something to us in our time of prayer: 

When you rise from meditation, remember the resolutions you have made, and, as occasion presents itself, carefully reduce them to practice that very day. This is the great fruit of meditation, without which it is not only unprofitable, but frequently hurtful: for virtues meditated upon, and not practiced often puff up the spirit, and make us imagine ourselves to be such as we have resolved to be.

Resolving: What can I specifically do to respond to what God has revealed to me in this passage? How can I carry this encounter with me into the day to influence how I think and act? 

A QUICK REFERENCE GUIDE FOR DISCOVERY PRAYER 

To make things easier for you, here is a simple guide for you to use for your daily prayer until it becomes second nature. 

Reading: An attentive, slow, leisurely, and repetitious reading of a short passage of the Bible 

Key Questions: What does the Bible text say in itself? What did the author intend? What does the Church teach about this subject? 

Reflecting: Prayerfully engaging with the meaning of the passage and considering how it may apply to your life circumstances 

Key Questions: What does this text say to me? How does it apply to my life? Where is God leading me? What is he revealing to me? 

Responding: Conversing with God about the passage 

Key Questions: What can I say in response to God? Should I offer thanksgiving or praise, or should I ask for his help in any particular way? 

Resting: Allowing yourself to rest and remain absorbed in the words of God, allowing or inviting the Holy Spirit to draw you more deeply into his presence through what you've read 

Key Questions: Am I being patient, attentive, and open to God's movement in my soul as I rest in his self - revelation? 

Resolving: Allowing the encounter with God to permeate your day, causing you to draw ever nearer to him through his self - revelation and invitation to participate with him in making his presence known in the world 

Key Questions: What can I specifically do to respond to what God has revealed to me in this passage? How can I carry this encounter with me into the day to influence how I think and act? Write down your resolutions and conclude with a prayer of thanksgiving. 

To sum up Discovery Prayer: Reading seeks, reflection finds, responding engages, resting tastes, and the person who experiences an authentic encounter with God resolves.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Follow the Discovery Prayer steps until they become natural.

-   Use the three keys (parts in red) to deepen your prayer time.

SIX: WINNING THE BATTLE OF PRAYER

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p 61-65

The monkeys represent everything that distracts you during prayer: your memory, your will, your emotions, your desires, your frustrations, your sins, your shortcomings, your interests, your habits, your good thoughts, your bad thoughts-all that makes you who you are. 

 Trick Them: When you are working to develop a new prayer habit, getting up fifteen to thirty minutes early can be one of the most challenging but effective ways to mitigate monkey business. Once you engage with an iThing or interact with too many people, the monkeys begin to wake up. Start early. 

Tolerate Them: Avoid self-condemnation or frustration. When you find yourself distracted, the best practice is to simply and gently turn your thoughts back to God. Thirty minutes of gentle but persistent turning back to God in prayer is worth more than a half hour of undisturbed bliss in which no exercise of self-discipline is required. Learn to relax. 

Train Them: It only takes about thirty days of consistent effort to get at least a majority of monkeys on board with a new habit or prayer program. After a month or so, your body begins to expect prayer and will even draw you to it. The first thirty days will likely be some of the hardest in your journey. Be persistent. 

Tame Them: Feed them with transformational truth. The food they require comes from your participation in the sacraments and prayer itself, but it also comes from the writings and lives of the saints. Follow the saints. 

Transform Them: This transformation comes about through 1) regular and attentive participation at Mass\-no less than every Sunday; 2) regular participation in the sacrament of penance\-no less than monthly; and 3) daily prayer and a life lived for God. Allow God to transform your heart and mind. 

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Trick the Monkeys, Start early.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Tolerate the Monkeys, Learn to relax

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Train the Monkeys, Be persistent.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Tame the Monkeys, Follow the saints.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Transform the Monkeys, Allow God to transform your heart and mind.

Experiencing God in Prayer

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p. 69-84

SEVEN: BE SCRAPPY (FEISTY)

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Read p. 87-90

Be "scrappy." (Webster's defines it as "having an aggressive and determined spirit: feisty."!) Rely upon God and pursue scrappiness, success follows. What do scrappy people look like? Here are a few characteristics as they relate to spirituality:

Be positive. Every obstacle they encounter is simply an indication that they need to find a way around it. 

Be resourceful. No matter what the obstacle, they never stand by helplessly; instead they immediately begin to look for solutions. By the grace of God, they almost never fail to find solutions, even if those solutions seem to be incomplete or imperfect. 

Be trusting. They know that God is in control, and that every obstacle is provided for their sanctification. 

Have a long view. They know that sometimes answers don't come in the short term, but the most important things in life are often only attained after long toil in the right direction. 

Be resilient. They never give up when it comes to the most important things in life. God will never ask more of them than they can handle; he will give them what they need to answer his high calling.

Be consistent. They get up every day and engage in prayer, no matter how they feel or what else is happening in their lives. 

Be humble. They are quick to seek help when they can't seem to find a way on their own.

Be scrappy, my friends, and you will know a joy and peace that you never thought possible.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Put these points up somewhere where you can be reminded of them, eg next to your desk, in your toilet.

-   ![unchecked](data:image/png;base64,)

    Put the scrappy principles into practice in your life.

Into the Deep: Checkpoints

Tick the cell for each started or completed checkpoint. Tasks just require a single tick. Skills and Habits should be started, but only completed once consolidated. In some cases this could take a month or more of practice. Don't rush it, persevere. 

Tasks: Tasks are once off things you do. You can revisit them if need be.

Skills: Skills are things that can take time to work on to master. Each one of these need to be mastered before moving on.

Habits: Habits take longer to develop and should be reflected on regularly to stay on track.

These three points are worth emphasising since they are crucial to long term growth.

The key is not to rush. The goal is not to finish a particular portion of Scripture --- or even to finish the steps laid out in Discovery Prayer --- but to purposefully delve into the depths of any passage that will draw your heart and mind to God. Said another way, this method is meant to orient your mind and heart to God, and you can set aside the method when this happens during any given time in prayer. p 46

Allow yourself to become absorbed in God's words as he invites you into a deeper kind of prayer, one that will bring you into his presence in ways that purely mental exercises could never achieve. You may not experience this kind of absorption as you initially explore this method of prayer. p 51

Resolving: What can I specifically do to respond to what God has revealed to me in this passage? How can I carry this encounter with me into the day to influence how I think and act? p 55

Once you have completed all the tasks you are ready for the next adventure: the Examen. You can still be working on your skills and habits while you start working on the Examen adventure.
